coredata.dc:title: Charge storage and memory effect in graphene quantum dots – PEG600 hybrid nanocomposite
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Mihalache, Iuliana
Radoi, Antonio
Munteanu, Cornel
Kusko, Mihaela
Kusko, Cristian
coredata.dc:description: We report an easy, one step, low cost method to obtain a hybrid composite material consisting in graphene quantum dots (GQDs) embedded in a polymeric – poly(ethylene glycol) bis (carboxymethyl) ether – matrix. Optical measurements show the excitation wavelength dependent photoluminescence of the GQDs – PEG600. In comparison with self-passivated GQDs, the composite exhibits a blue shifted photoluminescence, as well as additional emission peaks in the range of 570–600nm. These features are explained by the presence of new electronic surface states induced by the polymeric matrix as it was demonstrated by the electrochemical measurements. The transport properties consist in a large clockwise hysteresis presenting high and low resistance states, also two distinctive regions of negative differential resistance. The photocurrent decay and the transient currents indicate a large charge storage and confirm the existence of trap charge levels. The experimental findings suggest that the leading mechanism underling the transport is Simmons Verderber. We demonstrated the switching properties of GQDs – PEG600 for applications in non-volatile memory by performing standard sequence memory tests.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
PEG600 matrix
Electrical hysteresis
Non-volatile memory
Photoluminescence
Cyclic voltammetry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical and electrochemical investigations of self-passivated and PEG600 modified GQDs dispersions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Unitatea Executiva pentru Finantarea Invatamantului Superior, a Cercetarii, Dezvoltarii si Inovarii
Corporation for National and Community Service

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by CNCS–UEFISCDI Romania, under the project number PNII-ID-PCCE-0069/2011.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM image of GQDs in polymeric matrix; insets: the selected area FFT image (upper right) and diameter distributions of the GQDs (lower right).
Photoluminescence spectra of (a) self-passivated GQD and (b) GQD – PEG600 nanocomposite at different excitation wavelengths.
UV–Vis absorbance and photoluminescence emission spectra at λ ex =380nm, for bare-GQDs and PEG600 modified GQD samples.

Cyclic voltammograms for self-passivated and PEG600 modified GQDs recorded in TBAP as supporting electrolyte using a freshly cleaned Pt working electrode; inset graph-CV of Pt electrode in presence of supporting electrolyte.
(a) Schematic diagram of the planar device with a nanocomposite film drop-casted on top of the prepatterned interdigitated electrodes and (b) schematic energy diagram of the device.

I–V characteristic of GQD – PEG600 nanocomposite film deposited on interdigitated electrodes where the applied voltage was scanned in a loop from −20V to 20V and back to −20V, the arrows indicating the scan course. Inset: I–V curve in logarithmic scale of current.

(a) I–V characteristics at fixed scan rates for different ranges of applied voltage: −1→1→−1; −5→5→−5; and −10→10→−10, respectively; (b) I–V hysteresis at different scan rates (0.01V/s and 0.05V/s, respectively). (c) I–V characteristics at fixed scan rates for different ranges of applied voltage: −10→10→−10; 10→−10→10; 0→10→0, and 0→−10→0.
Transient currents obtained when the sample was polarized at the constant bias of 1V and 10V, respectively, for a fixed time and subsequently removed.
Time dependence rise and decay photoconductivity measurements at the biases of 0.1V and 1V respectively.
Operation of a write – read – erase – read sequence (−5/1/5/1V).

head.ce:title: Charge storage and memory effect in graphene quantum dots – PEG600 hybrid nanocomposite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Institute for Research and Development in Microtechnologies (IMT – Bucharest), 32B, Erou Iancu Nicolae Street, 72996 Bucharest, Romania

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Institute for Research and Development in Microtechnologies (IMT – Bucharest)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physics Department, University Bucharest, P.O. Box MG-11, 077125 Bucharest, Romania

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Physical Chemistry “Ilie Murgulescu”, 202 Spl. Independentei, 060021 Bucharest, Romania

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report an easy, one step, low cost method to obtain a hybrid composite material consisting in graphene quantum dots (GQDs) embedded in a polymeric – poly(ethylene glycol) bis (carboxymethyl) ether – matrix. Optical measurements show the excitation wavelength dependent photoluminescence of the GQDs – PEG600. In comparison with self-passivated GQDs, the composite exhibits a blue shifted photoluminescence, as well as additional emission peaks in the range of 570–600nm. These features are explained by the presence of new electronic surface states induced by the polymeric matrix as it was demonstrated by the electrochemical measurements. The transport properties consist in a large clockwise hysteresis presenting high and low resistance states, also two distinctive regions of negative differential resistance. The photocurrent decay and the transient currents indicate a large charge storage and confirm the existence of trap charge levels. The experimental findings suggest that the leading mechanism underling the transport is Simmons Verderber. We demonstrated the switching properties of GQDs – PEG600 for applications in non-volatile memory by performing standard sequence memory tests.

ce:section.ce:para: Colloidal quantum dots (QDs) and correspondingly solution processed electronic and optoelectronic devices offer a convenient alternative in terms of costs and physical flexibility compared to those realized by conventional epitaxial growth [1]. Accordingly, based on directly processing into thin films from solution, they have become promising materials for photonic components and devices, like ultrasensitive photodetectors in both visible and infrared wavelengths [2,3], and more recently for electrically driven memory devices [4,5].

In the recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have received a considerable attention due to their unique properties like size and excitation dependent photoluminescence (PL), electrochemical (redox transfer, photocatalysis) properties, combined with photobleaching resistance, biocompatibility and low toxicity. Therefore, the established color tunable photoluminescence along with other physical/chemical properties have demonstrated an auspicious application potential of carbon dots as active material in bioimaging [6], photocatalysis [7] and also for photonic [8] and electronic [9] devices.

Quasi spherical particles of graphitic or amorphous nature with the size less than 10nm have been produced by several approaches more or less complex depending on the time, cost or number of steps required [10]. Synthetic routes include laser ablation [11], arc discharge [12], electrochemical exfoliation of graphite [13], electron beam lithography [14] as top down methods, and thermal oxidation [15], supported synthesis [16] or, lately, microwave based methods [17–20] have been applied to suitable molecular precursors as bottom up methods. A facile approach to synthetize carbon based QDs consists in microwave assisted carbonization of saccharide [21], glycerol [22], chitosan [23], or citric acid [24], which act as carbon source leading to the formation of self-passivated carbon nanoparticles; consequently, the microwave pyrolysis might represents a green, low cost and rapid way of producing carbon dots. Although luminescence of self-passivated carbon dots occurs naturally due to the presence of functional groups on the surface, surface modification by directly pyrolyzing carbohydrates in the presence of passivation agents or by applying a multistep process subsequent to their synthesis, yields brighter and stronger fluorescence and allows adequate dispersion in different organic solvents [25]. Furthermore, it has been demonstrated that they have a graphitic structure, being essentially stacks of graphene sheets, which give the name ‘graphene quantum dots’ (GQDs) [26,27].

Different synthetic routes have been developed to generate polymer/QDs hybrid materials or to modify QDs surface [28], which include: (i) ligand exchange between polymer and QDs; (ii) grafting polymer to/from QDs; (iii) capping polymer onto QDs; or (iv) growing QDs within polymer template. Therefore, while simply blending polymers with nanoparticles typically leads to nanoparticle aggregation, highly impacting on their advantageous properties, finding a chemical route to obtain a new nanocomposite material, where both components have synergistic effect, has been proposed nowadays as an emerging research field. There are already reported methods of synthesis aiming to obtain functionalized CQDs with different polymeric chains, like poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG) [12] or polyethylenimine (PEI) [29], which are used mainly to tune the photo- and electroluminescence or to increase the quantum yield. Besides, the polymers themselves can be the source for CQDs or can be used in the synthesis process to obtain in a single step fluorescent functionalized CQDs [30,31].

Here we report a simple method to obtain hybrid graphene quantum dots – polymer (GQDs – PEG600) nanocomposites, by microwave assisted synthesis and we describe the corresponding opto-electrical properties of this nanomaterial. The high resolution transmission electron microscopy reveals well distinguished GQDs with a size distribution in the range of 3–5nm in diameter, which grew inside of the polymeric matrix, being evenly dispersed throughout the polymer. Both optical and electrical properties have been studied, aiming to evaluate the potential applications of the nanocomposite. Therefore, on one hand, the photoluminescence and photoconductivity measurements revealed a significant optical response, and, on the other hand, the I–V characterizations showed bipolar hysteretic response recommending this hybrid nanocomposite for organic memory devices development. Reproducibility of all results reported here was confirmed by testing nanocomposites from several different synthesis batches – the samples were stored at the room temperature and monitored for two months, showing stable optical and electrical properties. Therefore, the simple preparation method as well as the properties mentioned above made the microwave assisted synthesis a proper method for obtaining GQDs – PEG600 composite as a new hybrid material with potential of applications in plastic optoelectronics.

Poly(ethylene glycol) bis (carboxymethyl) ether (600 average molecular weight, with a theoretical length of a stretched molecule estimated to 4nm [32]) and d-(+)-glucose were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich. Chloroform was supplied by Carl Roth GmbH, acetonitrile was from Riedel-de Haën AG and tetrabutylammonium perchlorate was provided by Molekula GmbH. Platinum (Pt) disc electrodes, d =3.0mm, were supplied by Bioanalytical Systems (BAS, West Lafayette, USA).

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed on FEI Tecnai G2-F30 S-Twin field-emission gun scanning transmission electron microscope (FEG STEM) at an accelerating voltage of 300kV. A little amount of the carbon dots suspension was drop-casted on holey carbon-coated copper grids and subsequently air-dried before TEM analysis.

Absorption maxima were investigated with a Hitachi U-0080D UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at room temperature with an Edinburgh Instrument F920 spectrometer equipped with 450W Xenon lamp as excitation source; the fluorescence lifetimes were measured in time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) mode, using Nanosecond Pulsed Flash Lamp.

Electrochemical investigations were performed using Autolab/PGSTAT302N electrochemical system (Eco Chemie, The Netherlands), in 0.1M tetrabutylammonium perchlorate supporting electrolyte (TBAP) prepared in acetonitrile (ACN) and purged with nitrogen. Cyclic voltammetry measurements were performed with a three electrode configuration, i.e. a platinum disc working electrode, a platinum wire as counter electrode and Ag/AgNO3 reference electrode (RE-7 non-aqueous reference electrode from BAS Inc., Japan) in an acetonitrile solution including 0.01M AgNO3 and 0.1M TBAP.

The electrical characteristics of the CQD based nanocomposite were obtained using an interdigitated (IDT) electrode structure where 0.5μl of solution were drop-casted on the prepatterned IDT and then dried at room temperature. The planar device was fabricated on glass and it consisted of 13 pairs of Cr/Au (20/200nm) electrodes; they were 4μm wide, 200μm long, and the gap between adjacent digits is 4μm. The dc measurements were performed with the semiconductor characterization system Keithley 4200 SCS/C, connected to a dark Faraday cage. The photoresponse was recorded using AvaLight-D(H)-S light source, Avantes Inc. (USA), covering a spectral domain ranging between 215 and 2500nm. The halogen lamp supplied a power of 43μW, while the deuterium source was able to deliver 7μW, this being achieved by using optical fibers.

Self-passivated graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared accordingly to [10]. Glucose solution in distilled water (10%w/v) was subjected to a microwave assisted hydrothermal process, at 700W for 10min, and after that cooled at room temperature. Slightly changed protocol was applied for obtaining poly(ethylene glycol) bis (carboxymethyl) ether modified graphene quantum dots. Briefly 0.45g of d-(+)-glucose were solubilized in 2mL of distilled water and 2.5mL of poly (ethylene glycol) bis (carboxymethyl) ether were added. The homogenized solution (2.5mL in a 4mL glass bottle) was heated, for 10min at 700W, in a conventional microwave oven. For both types of carbon based quantum dots, a color change from transparent to yellow and to brownish, was visible by naked eye for self-passivated and modified ones, respectively. After cooling down at room temperature, self-passivated and modified quantum dots were extracted in chloroform. The chloroform was used only to extract from the reaction mixture the modified GQD, since glucose and water are not compatible with this organic solvent. This allowed us to work in water free conditions and avoid laborious purification steps. Chloroform is not interfering with electrical measurements since at room temperature is volatile and evaporates rapidly. To each 2.5mL of the reacted solutions two aliquots of CHCl3 (2.5mL each) were added, mixed vigorously and back collected. The self-passivated and modified graphene quantum dots chloroform based solutions were further used in our experiments.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) is generally used to ascertain size and crystallinity of a sample. Preferentially, we focused on the PEG600 modified quantum dots, since self-passivated graphene dots were previously reported and characterized [19]. Because of the polymer presence around the graphene dots (Fig. 1 ), it was hard to obtain well-resolved TEM images, nonetheless we could achieve size distribution (3–5nm) and average diameter (4nm) analysis.

Also, the lattice parameter of 0.33nm corresponding to the [002] facet, in agreement with the interlay distance of graphene sheets, demonstrating the graphitic nature of the crystalline dots [33]. Moreover, the fast Fourier transform diffraction pattern of red marked selected area and presented as inset in Fig. 1 revealed the [−110] and [1−10] planes of carbon [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and electrochemical investigations of self-passivated and PEG600 modified GQDs dispersions
ce:section.ce:para: Illuminated under UV light, both self-passivated GQDs and PEG modified GQDs samples exhibited bright green fluorescence, visible to human eye, while a sensitive detector was able to evidence the entire luminescence emission range covering from blue to red wavelengths under UV and visible excitation. As it is shown in Fig. 2 the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra shifted to longer wavelengths while varying excitation from 380 to 540nm. In the first set of experiments, where the PL spectra of self-passivated GQDs were recorded as function on different excitation wavelengths, a bathochromic effect with an average 80nm shift and a rapidly decrease in PL intensity were observed, a weak luminescence being recorded after 480nm excitation – Fig. 2(a). When the polymer is present, tuning the luminescence due to band gap modification induced by polymeric matrix embedment of GQDs was evidenced. The spectra shown in Fig. 2(b) reveal stronger and blue shifted PL bands, as well as the appearance of new bands at longer wavelengths, in the range of 500–650nm.

Although the photoluminescence is probably the most important feature of these recently discovered carbon nanostructures, its mechanism still needs to be elucidated [35]. Fig. 3 shows the absorption spectra recorded for the GQDs – PEG600 sample comparative with standard self-passivated GQDs sample. The resulting feature is a narrow, prominent peak at 275nm, due to the intense π–π * electronic transitions typically given by the extended π conjugation found on the graphene like structure of carbon nanoparticles, followed by a broad transition characterized by a shoulder around 320–370nm which extends down to the visible range of the spectrum. The maximum intensity of both self-passivated and PEG600 modified GQDs was observed at 380nm excitation, with a 40nm blue shift, and based on those photoluminescence maxima, the corresponding optical band gaps (Eg
, opt) were calculated to be 2.55eV, and 2.61eV, respectively. The presence of polymer matrix induces spectral shifts in the absorption and emission spectra of QDs and consequently the changes in the ligand environment can be also observed.

Luminescence lifetime was also recorded for GQDs – PEG600 composite (Fig. S1, Supporting Information) and a value of 7.3ns was determined by fitting with the third order exponential decay. Such value corresponds to a fast radiative recombination of excitons, similar with the results obtained in earlier reports [21,31]; the absolute quantum yield (QY) was also determined to be 2% at 380nm excitation wavelength.

As compared to optical spectroscopies, both photoluminescence and UV–Vis absorption, which provide only the difference between the band edges, the electrochemical methods allow solving the absolute positions of the band potentials, relative to the vacuum scale, necessary in developing further optoelectronic applications. Thus, cyclic voltammetry (CV) has been used to probe directly the effects of confinement on the highest occupied molecular-orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular-orbital (LUMO) energy levels, and to find the defect/surface state levels [36]. The onset-potentials of the oxidation and reduction peaks related to electron and hole injection into the conduction and valence bands were consequently used to determine the E HOMO (from E ox
,
onset) and E LUMO (from E red
,
onset) [37] using the following two equations:
(1)
E
HOMO
= - e ( 4.75 +
E
ox,onset
)
( eV )
(2)
E
LUMO
= - e ( 4.75 +
E
red,onset
)
( eV )

The Fig. 4 depicts the cyclic voltammograms of self-passivated GQDs, PEG600 modified GQDs, as well as the behavior of Pt electrode in presence of only supporting electrolyte – the inset graph. Self-passivated GQDs have a reduction peak (R GQDs) at −1.35V (vs. Ag/AgNO3), leading to an empirical LUMO energy E LUMO
,
GQDs =−3.65eV. Because the onset of oxidation of the self-passivated GQDs could not be accurately determined from the cyclic voltammetry shape related to the oxidation peak potential, we used the optical band gap to estimate E HOMO, GQDs =−6.2eV (E HOMO = E LUMO –Eg,
opt, where Eg,
opt ≈2.55eV).

PEG600 modified GQDs behaved differently from self-passivated GQDs, illustrating two supplementary reduction peaks shifted towards less negative values with respect to the reduction peak corresponding to LUMO level (R 1_GQDsPEG). Oxidation peak (OGQDsPEG) was observed at 1.67V, while the corresponding reduction peaks were situated at −1.37V (R 1_GQDsPEG), −0.96V (R 2_GQDsPEG) and −0.62V (R 3_GQDsPEG). The values for E HOMO, E LUMO calculated from these peaks’ onsets were E HOMO, PEG_GQDs =−6.25eV and E LUMO-1, PEG_GQDs =−3.6eV, respectively, leading to an electrochemical band gap Eg,ec
≈2.65eV. The other two reduction peaks were ascribed to supplementary LUMO levels situated E LUMO-2, PEG_GQDs =−3.9eV (for R2_GQDsPEG ) and E LUMO-1, PEG_GQDs =−4.35eV (for R 3_GQDsPEG).

Besides the morphological analyzes, the electrochemical evolutions of both self-passivated and polymer modified graphene based quantum dots showed similar behavior of the main reduction and oxidation peaks, probing that the presence of PEG600 did not influenced much on the energy levels positions and quantum dots microcrystallinity (size and structure). The observed supplementary peaks revealed that carbon dots embedded in the polymeric matrix, manifested additional surfaces states, thus inducing two more LUMO levels inside the bang gap.

Cyclic voltammetry experiments performed at different scan rates from 10 to 500mV/s were employed to evaluate the nature of the observed peaks, ascertaining a diffusional rather than an adsorptive process [38]. A linear relationship between peak current (I) and square root of scan rate (υ 1/2) was observed up to 100mV/s for self-passivated, as well as for PEG modified GQDs (Fig. S2, Supporting Information) indicating that the process is diffusion controlled and the electron transfer is taking place from GQDs to the electrode surface. It is worth to mention that in the case of R GQDs and R 1_GQDsPEG peak currents, even if the linearity was respected, the non-zero intercept of the current axis is evoking an electrochemical–chemical (EC) process, where both self-passivated and PEG coated carbon quantum dots transfer electrons to electrode through an intermediate which rapidly dissociates at the interface.

For a totally irreversible diffusion-controlled process, the dependence of the anodic/cathodic peak potentials on the logarithm of the scan rate can be used to calculate the overall electron transfer coefficient [39], characterizing the effect of the electrochemical potential on the electrochemical reaction activation energy:
(3)
E
p
=
2.303 RT
n α F
log ϑ + ct
where Ep -peak potential (V), R-universal gas constant (8.314JK−1 mol−1), F-Faraday constant (96,487C mol−1), T-Kelvin temperature (298K), n-number of electrons, α-charge transfer coefficient.

Thus illustrating peak potential positions for R 1_GQDs and R 1_GQDsPEG vs. logarithm of the scan rate (Fig. S3, Supporting Information), the obtained slopes, for the linear range, were −0.144 and −0.118, respectively. It was possible to calculate the charge transfer coefficient, assuming that one electron was involved, and in the case of self-passivated GQDs α was 0.18, while for GQDsPEG α was slightly higher, i.e. 0.22. Consequently, the electrochemical analysis revealed that the presence of the polymer had a reduced influence over quantum dots microcrystallinity, the overall electrode kinetic was a diffusion-controlled process, and LUMO electrons were extracted involving electron transfer.

The transport properties of the composite films polymer – GQD originate from the remarkable morphological and electronic properties of the GQD. Essentially, GQD are two dimensional or quasi two dimensional systems consisting in one graphene nanoflake or stacks of few graphene nanoflakes [19] leading to interesting electronic structure. Firstly, for the non-passivated GQD, the number of the edge states of these systems is on the same order of magnitude with the number of core states. Moreover, when the GQD are passivated with different types of functional groups (–OH, –COOH, etc.) the number of edge states is comparable with the number of core states [40]. Also the density of states is interesting, maintaining approximately the same form as the one of the infinite two – dimensional graphene, possibly presenting van Hove singularities [41], albeit for GQD a size dependent gap is present. Also, the form of the density of states is sensitive to the functional groups used as passivation agents. Besides the optical and electrochemical properties determined by the electronic structure, the large number of edge states indicates that GQD might possess a large number of trapping states. In terms of transport properties, these states determine the occurrence of hysteresis in the current–voltage characteristics, memory effects as well as charge storage.

Among the natural applications of GQDs based nanomaterials in organic optoelectronic devices like solar cells or light emitting diodes due to their accordable photoluminescence, their capacity to be used in organic memory devices has been evaluated by studying the electrical characteristics of the film.

Here we will explore these effects by performing three types of electrical measurements: (i) current–voltage characteristics, (ii) the time dependence of the transient charging and discharging currents, and (iii) time dependent rise and decay photoconductivity measurements. Finally, by exploiting the hysteresis properties of these systems we will investigate the possibility to apply the films of GQD – PEG in the realization of a two terminal nonvolatile memory device. The measurements were performed on the interdigitated structure described in the previous section, a schematic diagram of the planar device with a nanocomposite film drop-casted on top of the prepatterned interdigitated electrodes being shown in Fig. 5 (a). The material of choice for electrodes was gold since it has a large work function 5.1eV such that the Schottky barriers between gold and composite are minimized. The energy diagram of the device with the determined based on the electrochemical data are presented in Fig. 5(b). A metal-semiconductor-metal (MSM) type structure can be identified.

The dependence of the dark I–V characteristics on the direction of voltage sweeping is presented in Fig. 6 . The applied voltage was scanned in a loop from −20V to 20V and back to −20V, with a sweeping rate of 0.05V/s. In the figure the arrows indicate the scan course. The logarithm of the absolute value of current is presented in the inset graph. The recorded current density, calculated by assuming a cross section of a film thickness of 1μm and inter-electrode length of 5mm is in the range of −8 to +8mA/cm2. The nanocomposite material presents a nonlinear behavior, with large clockwise hysteresis in both bias regimes. The I–V characteristics are approximately symmetrical around the zero. The slight asymmetry is due to the fact that at the beginning of the sweep, the structure was not pre-polarized such that the current falls rather abruptly at biases around −20V. When a pre-polarization was applied before the voltage sweeping, the I–V curve was symmetrical around the origin, with the I–V curve for the reverse bias returning exactly at the starting point. There are several interesting features that can be distinguished in this characteristic. Firstly, after the abrupt drop of the current at the beginning of the measurements, the current decreases (in absolute value) approximately linearly for biases between −15V and −1V with a small change in slope occurring at −5V. When the voltage is approximately 2V, the I–V curve undergoes a significant change in slope, the current drops with more than two orders of magnitude, eventually crossing the horizontal line at the first open circuit voltage V oc =−0.85V. For positive biases, the I–V characteristic presents two regions of N-like shape negative differential region (NDR), the first one presenting the peak and the valley at 1.5V and 1.9V, respectively. For the first NDR region the peak valley ratio (PVR) is 2.2. The second NDR region has a peak at 4.3V and the valley at 5.7V with a PVR of 1.33. As the bias voltage increases, the current increases linearly, eventually presenting saturation for voltages above 10V. On the return curve, for positive biases, the current is smaller than the one of the forward curve (state OFF), it drops linearly, presenting the first change in slope at 4.5V and a second one around V oc =0.85V. Between 4V and 1V the current shows a broad hump-like feature. For the negative biases the reverse I–V characteristics is identical with the forward I–V characteristics at positive biases. The ratio between the current corresponding to the peak of the first NDR on the forward curve at positive bias and the current measured on the returning curve is approximately 50. The same ratio corresponding to the second NDR is approximately 10. The same values of these ratios are found for the negative biases.

We have also performed I–V loop characteristics for different bias ranges (−10V, +10V; −5V, +5V; and −1V, +1V). Fig. 7 (a) shows that the I–V curve for the bias (−5V, +5V) is perfectly overlapped on the curve recorded for the bias (−10V, +10V) although at the beginning of the voltage sweep the current drops abruptly as in the case of the bias range (−20V, +20V). As it was mentioned before, this effect is due to the initial polarization. For the smaller ranges of voltage biases, although the general form of the I–V curve resembles the one for the range (−20V, +20V), the well-defined peaks from +4.3V and −4.3V are smeared out becoming rather broad humps. Also the regions of NDR disappear. This indicates that electrical properties are sensitive to the history of the measurements and to the initial conditions. This fact is also illustrated in Fig. 7(b) where the dependence of the I–V characteristics as a function of sweeping rate is studied. For a sweeping rate of 0.01V/s the hysteresis is dramatically reduced, the reverse I–V curve being very close to the forward one. The absolute values of the currents are reduced with a factor of 3 and the features from ±1.5V and ±4.3V are smeared out. Here the characteristic is almost linear for absolute values of the voltages above V oc, and it resembles the characteristics of a standard MSM structure. This also indicates that charge storage phenomenon induced by the trapping states of GQD plays a significant role in determining the transport properties of the structure.

The Fig. 7(c) presents the I–V dual sweeps performed starting from the positive maximum voltage bias to the negative bias as well as I–V dual sweeps starting from 0V. We noticed that no major modifications of the electrical characteristics of the GQD – PEG nanocomposite occur. The I–V sweep from the −10V to 10V overlaps with the I–V sweep from +10V to −10V. This fact is expected since the interdigitated structure is symmetrical. Also the I–V sweep starting from 0V to positive voltages is the reflection of the I–V sweep from 0V to negative voltages, due to the same symmetry of the interdigitated structure. The difference between the complete I–V sweeps (negative voltages to positive voltages and reverse) and the I–V sweeps that start from 0V consists in the shapes of the first NDR regions. They become narrower albeit the PVR has approximately the same value as the ones occurring for the complete dual sweep (−10V to +10V).

This type of transport properties showing hysteresis, negative differential resistance, bistability or memristive behavior is frequently encountered in various structures based on nanoparticles embedded in organic [42] or inorganic matrices, inorganic/organic nanocomposites [43]. Moreover, these phenomena are present in various carbon based nanomaterial systems like carbon nanotubes [44,45] and graphene quantum dots [46].

We argue that for the system investigated in this work the most plausible scenario explaining the I–V characteristics presenting NDR regions is the one proposed by Simmons–Verdeber (SV) [47]. There are several other scenarios describing these type of transport properties such as (i) conduction filament formation [48,49], (ii) space charge limited current (SCLC) [50,51], and (iii) electric field induced charge transfer [52]. In the following, we will briefly discuss the applicability of these mechanisms for our systems. Firstly, due to the planar nature of the structure, the length of the conduction channel is 4μm rendering improbable the formation of the metallic filaments between the two electrodes induced by the gold ion migration into the composite film. Also for this length of the conduction channel the applied electric fields are with more than one order of magnitude lower than the ones present in the sandwiched structures ruling out the field induced charge transfer effect [53]. The space charge limited current model present a different type of I–V characteristics than the one recorded for our structures. In SCLC scenario [50], the I–V curve starts on the state with lower conductivity (OFF state) until the current increases rapidly, the system being in the state of higher conductivity. This type of I–V characteristic is opposite to the one measured for our systems where the I–V characteristics starts in the state with higher conductivity and on the return curve it is in the lower conductivity state.

It is known that PEG600 is a dielectric with a DC relative permittivity εr
=11.6 and a very low conductivity σ =10−9 Ω−1 cm−1 [54]. The effect of the polymer in this system is to introduce trapping levels in the band gap of the GQDs as it was shown by electrochemistry measurements presented in the previous section and illustrated in Fig. 5(b). Therefore, the following transport mechanism, similar to SV can be proposed in this case. As the voltage increases, the phenomenon of band bending occurs and the Fermi level sweeps the states inside the band gap. When the Fermi level matches the trapping states’ levels, the gold electrodes inject carriers into these, such that a space-charge electrical field opposing the applied one appears reducing the injection current, causing the appearance of the negative differential resistance. As the voltage increases further, the applied electric field overcomes the build-up space charge field and the current increases again. It is worth mentioning that similar behavior of the transport properties has been reported by Xie et al. [55], where the composite was AuNPs embedded in the insulating polymer (PDMS). On the reverse sweeping the internal field due to the charges trapped on the GQDs inhibits the injected current, the system being in the high resistance state (HRS).

As it will be shown further, the measurements of the charging and discharging transient currents and the time dependent rise and decay of the photoconductivity measurements indicate the existence of at least two trapping levels, one with relatively short lifetime and the other with long lifetime. Therefore, in this case, the charge trapping will be the leading component in the transport.

Fig. 8 shows the polarization and depolarization transient currents when trains of rectangular voltage pulses were applied to the structure. The upper panel of Fig. 8(a) shows the on – off sequence of voltage biases of 1V and 0V, respectively, and the lower panel shows the corresponding polarization depolarization currents. When a pulse of 1V and a duration of 100s is applied, the polarization current decays until it reaches a stationary state of 0.6nA. The time dependence of the polarization current can be fitted with a biexponential function with the time constants t 1 =1s and t 2 =9.3s (Fig. S4 (a1), Supplementary Material), times which can be correlated with charging of two electron trapping state near the nanocomposite LUMO, a deep level corresponding to the longer time constant and a shallow level corresponding to the shorter one. This behavior is in agreement with electrochemical measurements which also show the presence of additional states. When the 1V bias is removed for a duration of 500s, the structure is discharging. Fig. 8(a) shows the discharging current which is of opposite sign and can be also fitted with a biexponential with the time constants t 1 =0.8s and t 2 =12.4s – Fig. S4 (a2). These time constants correspond to the discharging of the trapping states. These measurements were repeated for voltage pulses of 10V – Fig. 8(b) – and the polarization and depolarization currents have the same behavior and biexponential functional dependence – Fig. S4 (b1) and (b2), respectively.

By integrating the experimental values of the current over time, one can calculate the total charged stored in the structure. Therefore, at the bias voltage of 1V the structure stores a charge of 10nC while at at the bias of 10V it stores approximately 122nC. The calculated capacitance of the interdigitated structure with the area of 0.2mm×0.2mm is 12.2nF at the voltage bias of 10V. This value corresponds to a capacitance per square centimeter of 25μF. The calculated capacitance of the interdigitated structure without the GQD based film is approximately 0.1pF which indicates the significant charge storage determined by the presence of GQDs. It is insightful to compare the values of the charge stored in our structures with the ones reported in the literature. These findings raise an interesting issue about the possibility to apply the GQD for charge storage devises.

The presence of the trapping states is also put in evidence by the time dependent rise and decay photoconductivity measurements. Here the structure biased with a voltage U=1V was illuminated by light pulses having a broad ultraviolet radiation. From Fig. 9 it can be seen that the structure presents a significant photoconductive response normalized to the dark current. The value of the dark current is 0.2nA and under illumination it raises by a factor of 1.5 to 0.3nA. We performed the same measurements at the bias of 0.1V in which case the photoresponse is higher, the photocurrent being with a factor of 2.75 higher than the dark current value. The structure does not present any response at the bias of 3V. It is noteworthy the rather long time of approximately 250s necessary for the current to reach the maximum value. After the light is turned off, the photocurrent decays, reaching after 250s its dark current value. As in the case of transient currents the rise and decay curves can be fit with a biexponential function (Fig. S5, Supplementary Information) whose time constants are t 1 =5s and t 2 =59s for current growth and t 1 =79s and t 2 =9s for its fall. These results indicate the existence of two different trapping states.

One of the natural applications of a material presenting electrical hysteresis or bistability is a nonvolatile memory device. Typically these devices are sandwich-like structures where a thin layer of polymer-nanoparticles composite [56], in particular graphene – polymer [57,46], is placed between two metallic electrodes (in some cases one electrode is realized from ITO). For the composite investigated in this work the hysteresis is strong even for the interdigitated structures where the electrodes are planar, therefore we investigated the memory behavior of these devices.

To understand the functionality of our memory device we will exploit the properties of the I–V characteristics, namely the two distinctive resistance states appearing at the forward and reverse scan. Quantitatively, the ratio between the current in the low resistive state and the one at the high resistance state at the bias ±4.3V is approximately 10. For a bias of ±1.5V this ratio is even higher of almost 100.

A standard sequence write-read-erase-read (WRER) was applied in order to investigate the switching properties of this device as it is shown in Fig. 10 . After the writing voltage pulse VW
=−5V was applied, the system was brought in the low resistance state (state ON) which was subsequently probed by applying the read voltage VR
=1V. The current measured when the read voltage probes the sample is I ON =100nA. To bring the system in the high resistance state (state OFF) an erasing voltage VE
=5V was applied. The reading voltage probes the state of the system indicating the low value of the current of I OFF =2 nA. For this structure, the ratio I ON/I OFF is approximately 50, a fairly large value which allows to avoid possible reading errors. The values reported in the literature for this ratio are approximately 103, our value being with a factor of 20 smaller. This is due to the planar nature of our memory structures which have the length of the conduction channel of 4mm. The memory tests performed here had the aim to explore the potential of this PEG600 – GQD composite for applications in non-volatile memory devices. We also tested the endurance of the memory structure by performing 100 operating WRER sequences. The results are reproducible with no variation of the value of the currents.

In conclusion we demonstrated that the microwave assisted pyrolysis is a simple low-cost method capable of obtaining a PEG600 – GQDs hybrid composite with interesting optical and electrical properties.

PL and UV–Vis measurements revealed a band gap of 2.55eV and tunable photoluminescence with the excitation wavelength, a characteristic feature of the GQDs in particular, and CQDs in general. The optical measurements were in good agreement with electrochemistry investigations which gave the energy values of the HOMO and LUMO levels, as well as for the trapping levels given by the interactions of GQDs with polymeric matrix.

Electrical measurements indicated that the transport phenomena are dominated by the charge storage in the trapping levels, leading to large hysteresis and two distinctive NDR regions. The charge accumulated on the test structures was 0.12μC for a bias voltage of 10V, leading to a total capacitance of 12nF or a capacitance per unit area of 25μF/cm2. These findings suggest potential applications of GQD based materials for charge storage devices. Also, a significant photoconduction was found for ultraviolet illumination.

Finally, exploiting the large hysteresis and the occurrence of two distinctive conductivity states we explored the possibility to use this nanocomposite for non-volatile memory devices. We performed memory tests and we have demonstrated that the simplest planar structure (obtained by drop casting composite solution on pre-patterned interdigitated electrodes) exhibits switching properties.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by CNCS–UEFISCDI Romania, under the project number PNII-ID-PCCE-0069/2011.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.orgel.2013.10.031.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bulletin of the Institute for Chemical Research
